replies.author: @snappertrx
replies.text:
​@Nedster442Correct. Working 40 hours a week is inconsequential. What is the job? It's basic supply and demand. If you're pushing a broom then guess what? A million other people can do your job, there's no need of special training or schooling. The pay is low. If you're an electrician you've had to go through school, gotten special training and certification. You are now 1 of 1000 instead of 1/1 million. Your demand is higher, your pay is higher.
Pushing a broom is something you have a 16 year old kid do because he wants gas money and needs to learn to wake up and get to work on time. If you're paying that kid the same as the electrician you're going to cause your business to go broke AND your going to devalue what you pay the electrician. Now everyone loses. We are literally living this out in realtime right now thanks to minimum wage laws.

They went overseas. Computers took them over. No one bags groceries or helps you get them to your car. Half the time we check out our own groceries. No one pumps your gas. There are no paid school cross walk guards. That whole under layer of jobs are gone along with true customer service. The person who posted a comment to you and said his wife has a nice office job, said it was in a different country so what does that mean to me? Plus, office jobs were supposed to pay the rent. They were the jobs you worked up to. The real truth is that all the jobs I could have reasonably gotten when I was young, (I'm 66) are gone. It's been coming for a long time that the young and old will compete for the same low wage jobs. Also, the recently laid off or handicap. This world can be like when half of Twitter was gone in an instant and it still kept running. This is where we are. That's why I have to live on $800/ mo somehow. I don't know the answers, I just know that we keep talking like it's coming when it's already here. Most jobs I have done over my lifetime were to get something ready to be automated so that I was expendable. And I mostly did jobs that were barely a step up from entry level. I'm glad I don't have to do it over again. I know there are smart people out there. Please find the solution. Also, those who complain that kids these days don't leave the parents house, it's just a symptom of the issue that families need to be together longer or always just to have a home. Sorry this was long. It's a sore spot with me when someone who doesn't have to compete in this market for a job weighs in on it but doesn't know anything about the subject. Thank you for your comment.

author: @csumme7
text: One of the first jobs I had at 17 was a Churches Chicken back in the mid 80s. Worked my a55 off for 3.35 an hour, 60 bucks a week. Had to get a roommate to split the rent at an apartment and we lived off powdered milk, government cheese, and eggs (breakfast lunch and dinner with eggs scrambled, poched, sunnyside up, every damn combination). Yet we still lived and had our freedom to ride our pawn shop 10 speed bikes (all we could afford at the time) around time. Interesting how they scream for a "living wage" to flip burgers today.
